articles.label: Legal
articles.headline: Pensacola's Short-Term Rental Fight Heads to Court
articles.dek: A group of downtown property owners filed suit this week challenging the city's new permitting caps. The case could reshape how Pensacola regulates vacation rentals for years.
brief: Seven downtown property owners filed suit in Escambia County Circuit Court challenging Pensacola's short-term rental permit cap enacted in January
The lawsuit argues the city's 150-permit limit for the downtown core constitutes an unconstitutional taking of property rights
A hearing on the plaintiffs' request for injunctive relief is scheduled for May 12
articles.body:
The lawsuit landed on Monday morning. By noon, phones were ringing at City Hall.

Seven downtown Pensacola property owners filed a complaint in Escambia County Circuit Court challenging the city's four-month-old short-term rental ordinance, arguing the permit cap violated their constitutional property rights and was enacted without adequate public notice.

The case, Palafox Properties LLC v. City of Pensacola, names the city and City Council members in their official capacities. It seeks injunctive relief blocking enforcement of the cap and unspecified monetary damages.

"This isn't about one property or one owner," said Robert Kimball, an attorney with the Pensacola firm Beggs & Lane representing the plaintiffs. "This is about whether the city can simply decide your property is worth less because they don't like how you use it."

What the Ordinance Does

Pensacola City Council passed the short-term rental ordinance in January on a 5-2 vote after nearly a year of contentious public meetings. The law caps permits at 150 for the downtown core—roughly the area bounded by Cervantes Street, A Street, the bay, and I-110. It requires annual inspections, proof of liability insurance, and a local property manager available around the clock.

Properties operating as short-term rentals before January 1, 2025, were grandfathered in. Everyone else goes on a waiting list.

That waiting list is now 43 names long.

City Council member Allison Chen, who sponsored the ordinance, declined to comment on pending litigation. In a written statement, she said the city "remains confident the ordinance represents a reasonable exercise of local regulatory authority."

The Plaintiffs' Argument

According to the complaint, four of the seven plaintiffs purchased downtown properties between March and November 2024 with the explicit intention of operating short-term rentals. They cite text messages and emails with real estate agents, inspection reports, and renovation contracts.

When the ordinance passed, their permit applications were denied. The cap had already been reached.

"These owners did their due diligence," Kimball said in an interview. "They bought properties the city had allowed to operate as rentals for years. Then the city changed the rules after the fact."

The lawsuit makes three primary claims: that the permit cap constitutes a regulatory taking under the Fifth Amendment, that the city failed to provide constitutionally adequate notice before the January vote, and that the ordinance's enforcement has been arbitrary and inconsistent.

On the notice claim, the complaint points to a city website posting that listed the wrong meeting date for the final vote. The error was corrected 48 hours before the meeting.

The City's Position

City Attorney Marcus Webb said in a brief statement that the city would "vigorously defend" the ordinance. He declined to discuss legal strategy.

Past city statements have framed the cap as a quality-of-life measure. Residents of the North Hill and East Hill neighborhoods packed public meetings throughout 2024, describing party houses, parking problems, and transient crowds that eroded the character of historic blocks.

"I didn't move to a hotel district," North Hill resident Patricia Sandoval told the council in November. "I moved to a neighborhood."

The city's legal filings are due by May 5. A hearing on the plaintiffs' motion for preliminary injunction is set for May 12 before Circuit Judge David Williams.

Broader Implications

How Williams rules could affect more than Pensacola's downtown. Similar battles have played out in Destin, St. Augustine, and Key West. Florida's legislature has repeatedly considered—and rejected—bills that would preempt local short-term rental regulation entirely.

"This is the tension point in Florida right now," said Sarah Nguyen, a land use professor at Florida State University's College of Law who is not involved in the case. "Property rights versus community character. Home rule versus state preemption. These cases are going to keep coming until Tallahassee or the courts settle it."

For now, the 150 permitted downtown rentals continue to operate. The 43 applicants on the waiting list continue to wait.

And seven property owners are betting a judge will see things their way.

articles.label: WATERFRONT
articles.headline: Port of Pensacola's Cruise Terminal Gamble
articles.dek: City officials are betting $12 million on a renovated terminal to attract more cruise ships. Maritime experts say the math doesn't add up.
brief: Pensacola City Council approved $12 million for cruise terminal renovations despite hosting only 8 cruise calls in 2025
Maritime industry analysts say the port lacks the depth, infrastructure and population base to compete with Mobile and New Orleans
City projects 40 annual cruise calls by 2030, but has no signed agreements with major cruise lines
articles.body:
The warehouse at 700 South Barracks Street smells like rust and seawater. Paint peels from steel beams that haven't supported cargo operations in years. A forklift with flat tires sits near the loading dock, next to pallets of who-knows-what covered in blue tarps.

This is where Pensacola plans to welcome 150,000 cruise passengers annually by 2030.

In February, the Pensacola City Council voted 6-1 to allocate $12 million for renovating the port's cruise terminal facilities. The project includes a new passenger processing building, covered walkways, restroom facilities and what city documents describe as a "world-class arrival experience."

The problem, according to maritime industry analysts and cruise line executives who spoke with The Flightline, is that almost nothing about Pensacola's port makes it attractive to the modern cruise industry.

The Numbers Don't Float

In 2025, the Port of Pensacola hosted eight cruise ship calls. Seven were from American Cruise Lines' small-ship coastal vessels carrying fewer than 200 passengers each. One was a repositioning stop from Carnival, which used Pensacola as an emergency port after weather diverted a ship bound for Mobile.

Compare that to Mobile, 60 miles east, which hosted 104 cruise calls last year. Or New Orleans, which saw 287.

"Pensacola is competing for table scraps in a market that's already oversaturated," said Derek Robbins, a maritime logistics consultant based in Houston who has advised Gulf Coast ports on cruise operations. "The major lines have committed to Mobile and New Orleans through 2032. They're not looking for another regional port."

The city's feasibility study, conducted by Bermello Ajamil & Partners in 2024, projected 40 annual cruise calls by 2030 if terminal improvements were completed. The study cited Pensacola's "authentic Gulf Coast charm" and "proximity to white-sand beaches" as competitive advantages.

It did not address the port's 32-foot channel depth, which prevents most modern cruise ships from docking. The Carnival Vista, a mid-sized vessel that represents the industry's current standard, requires 27 feet of draft. At low tide, Pensacola's channel runs closer to 29 feet—leaving almost no margin for error.

"You're one bad tide away from grounding a ship with 4,000 passengers," Robbins said. "No cruise line risk management department is going to approve that."

What the City Says

Pensacola Mayor D.C. Reeves has championed the cruise terminal project since taking office in 2023. At the February council meeting, he called it "a transformational investment in our maritime future."

"We're not trying to be Mobile or New Orleans," Reeves said in an interview last week. "We're positioning ourselves for the small-ship and expedition cruise market, which is the fastest-growing segment of the industry."

The small-ship market does exist. American Cruise Lines and Viking have expanded coastal itineraries in recent years. But these vessels carry between 100 and 350 passengers—a fraction of the 150,000 annual visitors the city is projecting.

To hit that number with small ships alone, Pensacola would need roughly 600 cruise calls per year. That's more than twice Mobile's current volume.

Amy Tharpe, the city's director of maritime operations, acknowledged the projections rely heavily on attracting at least one major cruise line to establish Pensacola as a seasonal home port.

"We're in active discussions with several operators," Tharpe said. She declined to name them, citing confidentiality agreements.

When pressed on whether any cruise line has signed a letter of intent or memorandum of understanding with the city, Tharpe said no.

"These negotiations take time," she said. "The terminal investment signals to the industry that we're serious."

Where the Money Goes

The $12 million allocation breaks down as follows: $4.2 million for the passenger terminal building, $2.8 million for site work and utilities, $1.9 million for covered walkways and canopies, $1.6 million for parking improvements, and $1.5 million for "soft costs" including architecture, engineering and permitting.

The money comes from the city's general fund reserves, not dedicated port revenue. Pensacola's port operations lost $340,000 in fiscal year 2025, according to budget documents. The port has operated at a deficit in eight of the last ten years.

Council member Allison Patton cast the lone dissenting vote in February. She questioned whether the investment made sense given the port's track record.

"I've sat through three different presentations about cruise tourism in six years," Patton said during the meeting. "Each one promises we're on the verge of a breakthrough. Each time, the ships don't come."

Patton requested that city staff provide monthly updates on cruise line negotiations. The council approved that request unanimously.

The Depth Problem

Even if a cruise line wanted to make Pensacola a home port, the channel depth issue would require federal intervention.

The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers maintains the Pensacola shipping channel. Deepening it to 35 feet—the minimum standard for large cruise vessels—would cost an estimated $180 million to $240 million, according to a 2019 Corps feasibility study. The project would require Congressional authorization and a local cost share of at least 35 percent.

Escambia County has shown no interest in participating. County Commissioner Jeff Bergosh, whose district includes the port area, called the cruise terminal project "a city problem" at a commission meeting in March.

"If they want to dredge the channel, they can find the money themselves," Bergosh said. "The county has roads to fix."

The city's cruise terminal plans do not include any funding for channel improvements. When asked how the port would accommodate larger ships without dredging, Tharpe said the city was "focused on the facilities we can control."

What Happens Next

Construction on the terminal improvements is scheduled to begin in October. The city has issued a request for qualifications for architecture and engineering firms, with responses due May 15.

Meanwhile, the eight-ship 2026 cruise season has already been booked. All eight calls are American Cruise Lines vessels. No other cruise line has scheduled a Pensacola stop.

In the warehouse at 700 South Barracks Street, the forklift hasn't moved. The tarps are still there. The paint keeps peeling.

Pensacola is betting $12 million that someone will eventually sail into this port and see something worth stopping for.

The cruise industry, so far, has not.

articles.label: Military
articles.headline: Blue Angels Homecoming Week Begins With Traffic Questions Unanswered
articles.dek: The July air show draws 150,000 spectators annually. FDOT and county officials still haven't agreed on a traffic plan with 11 weeks to go.
brief: The Blue Angels Homecoming Air Show is scheduled for July 11-12 at NAS Pensacola, with an estimated 150,000 attendees expected over two days.
FDOT District 3 and Escambia County have not finalized agreements on traffic control, signal timing, or contraflow lanes on Blue Angel Parkway.
NAS Pensacola's public affairs office says the base will release parking and entry procedures by June 1, leaving private shuttle operators uncertain about pickup locations.
articles.body:
The marquee outside Pensacola Beach's Flounders advertises $8 oysters and Blue Angels drink specials. A banner on Palafox promotes hotel packages for "Homecoming Week 2026." The tourism machine is already humming for the July air show.

Eleven weeks out, nobody has agreed on how 150,000 people will get there.

The Blue Angels Homecoming Air Show runs July 11-12 at NAS Pensacola. It is the largest annual event in Escambia County by attendance. Last year's show drew an estimated 78,000 spectators on Saturday alone, according to base public affairs. The roads around the west side of Pensacola—Blue Angel Parkway, Navy Boulevard, Barrancas Avenue—absorbed that crowd with a traffic plan that took six months to coordinate.

This year's plan doesn't exist yet.

The Sticking Points

Florida Department of Transportation District 3 controls Blue Angel Parkway, the primary artery connecting Interstate 10 to the base's west gate. Escambia County manages the secondary routes and most intersection signals. NAS Pensacola controls access to the base itself and coordinates with the Navy's security requirements.

Three entities. Three sets of priorities. No signed agreement.

"We've had preliminary conversations," said FDOT District 3 spokesperson Marcus Whitfield. "We're still waiting on the county to confirm their signal timing preferences and personnel availability."

Escambia County Public Works Director Janet Simmons said her department submitted timing proposals in March. "We sent those over weeks ago. We're waiting on FDOT to approve the contraflow plan."

The contraflow plan would reverse northbound lanes on Blue Angel Parkway to create four southbound lanes during peak arrival hours Saturday morning. Last year, this reduced backups to I-10 by approximately 40 minutes, according to county traffic data. Without it, vehicles idled on the interstate shoulder for nearly two miles.

Neither agency would say what specific disagreements remain unresolved.

The Base's Timeline

NAS Pensacola Public Affairs Officer Lieutenant Commander Rachel Okonkwo said the base plans to release its official parking and entry procedures by June 1.

"We're working closely with our partners in county and state government," Okonkwo said in an emailed statement. "The safety of our guests and the surrounding community is our top priority."

June 1 is six weeks before the show. For private shuttle operators, that timeline creates problems.

Mike Treadwell runs Gulf Coast Event Transport, a charter bus company that ferried over 3,000 passengers to last year's air show from remote parking lots at Cordova Mall and the Pensacola Bay Center. He said he needs confirmed pickup and dropoff locations by mid-May to finalize contracts with corporate clients.

"These are companies booking 50, 60 seats at a time," Treadwell said from his office on Fairfield Drive. "They want to know where their people are getting picked up. They want to know it's actually going to happen. I can't tell them anything right now."

Treadwell said he's called NAS Pensacola's public affairs office three times since March. Each time, he was told to wait for the June announcement.

What Worked Last Year

The 2025 traffic plan relied on four components: contraflow on Blue Angel Parkway from 7 a.m. to 11 a.m. Saturday, temporary signal priority for southbound traffic on Navy Boulevard, a designated rideshare staging area on the base's northwest perimeter, and motorcycle-only parking near Gate 1.

Escambia County Sheriff's Office provided 34 deputies for traffic control. FDOT supplied electronic message boards along I-10 and I-110. The Navy restricted left turns at three intersections inside the base perimeter.

By most accounts, it worked. Social media complaints focused on the heat and concession lines, not the drive in.

"Last year was the smoothest it's been in probably five years," said Pensacola City Council member Casey Harper, whose district includes neighborhoods west of NAS Pensacola. "People weren't parking in yards. People weren't blocking driveways. That's what we heard from residents."

Harper said he's received no briefings on this year's plan. He learned about the FDOT-county disagreement from a constituent who works at the county public works department.

"That's not great," Harper said. "We should probably know what's going on."

The Tourism Stakes

Visit Pensacola estimates Blue Angels Homecoming generates $12 million in direct visitor spending. Hotels within five miles of the base sell out months in advance. Restaurants along the Pensacola Beach corridor report their highest-revenue weekend of the summer.

"This is our Super Bowl," said Darryl Chen, general manager of the Hilton Pensacola Beach. "We've been booked since February."

Chen said guests ask about transportation when they call to confirm reservations. His staff currently tells them to check the NAS Pensacola website closer to the event.

"It's not ideal," Chen said. "People want to plan."

Visit Pensacola spokesperson Amanda Reyes declined to comment on the traffic coordination delays, saying the organization defers to government agencies on operational matters.

What Happens Next

FDOT's Whitfield said his agency expects to finalize the traffic plan by mid-May. Escambia County's Simmons said the same.

Neither would commit to a specific date.

The Blue Angels themselves are not involved in ground transportation planning. The squadron handles flight demonstrations, community engagement, and air operations. Everything that happens on the roads belongs to someone else.

For now, the oyster specials and hotel packages keep selling. The banners stay up on Palafox. July 11 is 84 days away.

The route to get there remains under discussion.
